title: Hyperplanes of Squier’s cube complexes
overall_summary: The paper studies Squier cube complexes S(P,w) associated to semigroup presentations P and base words w, focusing on their hyperplanes. Its central contributions are explicit descriptions of when two Squier hyperplanes coincide or cross, an ordered relation ≺ on crossing hyperplanes whose chains exactly correspond to cubes, criteria for specialness, and decompositions of Squier complexes by cutting along well-chosen clean disjoint hyperplanes. The most transferable material for the matching complex F_w is the hyperplane technology: classify local moves up to context, order transverse hyperplanes by planar/linear position, prove chain-to-cube and transitivity statements, and then use CAT(0) cubulation, product-of-trees embeddings, or graph-of-spaces decompositions. The paper does not discuss the free-group matching complex directly, but it provides a detailed model for proving contractibility of a cubical complex built from local resolutions by showing it is CAT(0), or by inductively cutting along product-neighborhood hyperplanes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.2 and Theorem 2.4 (cubical CAT(0) facts recalled)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A cube complex is nonpositively curved if the link of every vertex is a simplicial flag complex; in the cubical setting recalled in the paper, a simply connected nonpositively curved cube complex is CAT(0). If X is a CAT(0) cube complex, then X is special in the sense of Haglund and Wise, and every hyperplane of X separates X into two connected components called halfspaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes these as standard facts, citing Bridson-Haefliger for the nonpositive curvature/CAT(0) criterion, Haglund-Wise for specialness of CAT(0) cube complexes, and Sageev for hyperplane separation. The operational content used later is that CAT(0) rules out hyperplane pathologies and gives a halfspace pocset: each hyperplane has exactly two complementary sides, so vertices can be encoded by choices of sides.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.12 (cubulating a pocset)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Sigma,<,*) be a pocset: a partially ordered set with an involution * such that A and A* are never comparable, and A < B if and only if B* < A*. An ultrafilter alpha chooses exactly one of A and A* for each A and is upward closed in the sense that B < A and B in alpha imply A in alpha. A DCC ultrafilter is one in which every infinite descending chain is eventually constant. Construct a cube complex X(Sigma) whose vertices are DCC ultrafilters, whose edges join ultrafilters differing on exactly one complementary pair, and whose higher cubes are filled whenever their 1-skeletons appear. Then every connected component of X(Sigma) is a CAT(0) cube complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as Sageev-Roller cubulation and does not reprove it. The construction makes a cube whenever a finite set of choices can be flipped independently. The DCC condition selects the vertices in the intended component. The flag condition in links is built into the rule that every compatible family of pairwise independent flips spans a cube, and the standard cubulation theorem gives simply connectedness of components and hence CAT(0).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.14 (distance after cubulating selected hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a CAT(0) cube complex, let H be a collection of hyperplanes of X, and let X(H) be the cube complex obtained by cubulating the pocset of halfspaces delimited by hyperplanes in H. For a vertex v of X, let phi(v) be the principal ultrafilter consisting of all halfspaces in this pocset that contain v. Then for any vertices x,y of X, the combinatorial distance in X(H) between phi(x) and phi(y) is exactly the number of hyperplanes in H that separate x from y in X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In a CAT(0) cube complex, combinatorial distance between two vertices equals the number of hyperplanes separating them. The two principal ultrafilters phi(x) and phi(y) differ precisely for those hyperplanes of H whose two halfspaces contain x and y on opposite sides. In the cubulated complex, crossing one edge flips exactly one such choice, so the graph distance is the number of differing choices, i.e. the number of selected hyperplanes separating x and y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.15, Theorem 2.18, and Lemma 2.20 (Squier complexes, Farley complexes, and universal covers)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = <Sigma | R> be a semigroup presentation and w a base word in Sigma+. The Squier complex S(P,w) is the connected component of the Squier complex whose vertices are words equal to w modulo P, edges apply one defining relation inside a context, and n-cubes apply n relations in disjoint contexts. The diagram group D(P,w) is naturally isomorphic to pi_1(S(P),w). The Farley complex X(P,w) has vertices reduced semigroup diagrams Delta with top(Delta)=w, and its n-cubes are spanned by diagrams Delta·P where P ranges over prefixes of a thin diagram with n cells. Farley's theorem states that X(P,w) is a complete CAT(0) cube complex. The map Delta ↦ bot(Delta) induces the universal covering X(P,w) → S(P,w), and the quotient X(P,w)/D(P,w) is cellularly isomorphic to S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A loop in S(P) based at w is a finite sequence of relations that starts and ends at w; stacking the corresponding atomic semigroup diagrams gives a spherical diagram. The squares and higher cubes of S(P) encode commutations of independent relations, so the homotopy class of the loop matches the reduced spherical diagram, giving D(P,w) ≃ pi_1(S(P),w). Farley's complex is built by allowing independent atomic diagrams to be attached simultaneously; its cube structure records the Boolean lattice of prefixes of a thin diagram. The CAT(0) property is quoted from Farley. Left multiplication by spherical diagrams gives a free action of D(P,w) on X(P,w), and taking bottom labels forgets the chosen lift but records exactly the Squier vertex and cube, producing the quotient and universal cover.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (equality of Squier hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (p,a→b,q) and (r,c→d,s) be oriented edges in the Squier complex S(P), where a=b and c=d are defining relations in the displayed orientations. Denote by [p,a→b,q] and [r,c→d,s] the oriented hyperplanes dual to these edges. Then [p,a→b,q] = [r,c→d,s] if and only if a=c and b=d as words in Sigma+, and p=r and q=s modulo P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward implication, take a path of parallel edges in the equivalence class defining the hyperplane. Induct on the length of this path. Passing from one edge to a parallel edge across a square only changes the context on the left or the right by applying a relation in P; the distinguished relation a→b itself is unchanged. Thus after any number of square moves the relation word is still a→b and the left and right contexts are only changed modulo P. For the converse, choose derivation sequences from p to r and from q to s modulo P. Applying these derivations in the left and right contexts of the fixed central edge (a→b) produces a grid of squares showing that the two displayed edges are connected by parallelism, hence dual to the same oriented hyperplane.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 and Remark 3.3 (transversality and the order ≺)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a,u→v,b) and (c,p→q,d) be edges of S(P). The hyperplanes [a,u→v,b] and [c,p→q,d] are transverse if and only if there exists a word y in Sigma+ satisfying either c = a u y and b = y p d modulo P, or d = y u b and a = c p y modulo P. Moreover, in the first alternative, the paper's relation [a,u→v,b] ≺ [c,p→q,d] holds: the first hyperplane is the one corresponding to the relation applied to the left subword in a square.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the displayed congruences hold, one writes down the corresponding square: in the first case the two relations occur as disjoint subwords in the word a u y p d, with u→v on the left and p→q on the right; in the second case the roles are reversed. Lemma 3.1 identifies the hyperplanes dual to the sides of this square with the originally specified hyperplanes. Conversely, if two hyperplanes cross, they cross in some square (x,p→q,y,u→v,z) or (x,u→v,y,p→q,z). Applying Lemma 3.1 to compare the sides of this square with the given edges gives exactly one of the two systems of congruences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5 (product form of a Squier hyperplane neighborhood)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For words a,u,v,b with u=v a defining relation, let J = [a,u→v,b] be an oriented hyperplane in S(P). Let S(P,a) u S(P,b) denote the image of S(P,a) × S(P,b) under the map (alpha,beta) ↦ alpha u beta, and similarly define S(P,a) v S(P,b). With the orientation induced by u→v, the negative boundary component of the hyperplane neighborhood is ∂−J = S(P,a) u S(P,b), and the positive boundary component is ∂+J = S(P,a) v S(P,b). If J is clean, then J is naturally isometric to S(P,a) × S(P,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lift J to a hyperplane in the Farley complex. A cube in the carrier of the lift consists of the distinguished cell changing u to v, together with any collection of independent cells occurring strictly in the prefix a or suffix b. Therefore the two boundary components of the carrier are obtained by allowing arbitrary Squier moves in the prefix and suffix while keeping the middle word u or v fixed. Passing down to S(P) via the quotient X(P,w)/D(P,w) gives the stated descriptions. If J is clean, the quotient does not identify the two sides or fold the carrier along itself, so the mid-hyperplane is exactly the product of the prefix and suffix Squier complexes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.8, 3.9, and 3.10 (local hyperplane pathologies in Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In any Squier complex S(P), every hyperplane is 2-sided. A hyperplane J is self-intersecting if and only if J = [a,p→q,c] for some words a,p,q,b,c with p=q a defining relation and with a = a p b and c = b p c modulo P. A hyperplane J is self-osculating if and only if J = [a,(k h)^n k → p,b] for some n ≥ 1 and words a,k,h,p,b, where the displayed arrow is a defining relation, such that a = a k h and b = h k b modulo P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For 2-sidedness, if a hyperplane were 1-sided, one would have [a,x→y,b] = [a,y→x,b], contradicting Lemma 3.1 because defining relations are not of the form x=x and the orientation data would have to agree. For self-intersection, one direction is explicit: if a = a p b and c = b p c modulo P, then Lemma 3.1 makes J dual to two nonparallel sides of the square (a,p→q,b,p→q,c). Conversely, any self-intersection occurs in such a square, and Lemma 3.1 forces the same relation to label both crossing directions and gives the displayed congruences. For self-osculation, two distinct edges dual to the same oriented hyperplane share an endpoint but do not span a square. The two occurrences of the same relation overlap; writing the overlap equation as u v = v w and choosing the maximal power of u that prefixes v yields u = k h, v = (k h)^n k, and w = h k. Lemma 3.1 then converts equality of hyperplanes into the congruences a = a k h and b = h k b. The converse builds the two osculating edges with common endpoint from these congruences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6 and Corollary 3.7 (criteria for clean and special Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = <Sigma | R> be a semigroup presentation and w0 in Sigma+ a base word. The following are equivalent: (i) every hyperplane of S(P,w0) is clean; (ii) S(P,w0) has no self-intersecting hyperplanes; (iii) there do not exist words a,b,p in Sigma+ such that w0 = a b, a = a p, and b = p b modulo P, with the congruence class [p]_P not equal to the singleton {p}. Moreover, S(P,w0) is special if and only if these equivalent conditions hold and, in addition, there do not exist words a,u,v,w,b,p,q,xi in Sigma+ such that w0 = a u v w b, au = au(v xi), wb = (xi v)wb modulo P, and uv=p and vw=q are defining relations in R. Corollary 3.7 states that if no words a,b,p satisfy w0=ab, a=ap, and b=pb modulo P, then S(P,w0) is special; in particular, if the congruence class [w0]_P is finite, then S(P,w0) is special.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication clean implies no self-intersection is immediate. If condition (iii) fails, choose a nontrivial relation inside p, say p=x r y with r=s in R; then the hyperplane [a x,r→s,y b] self-intersects inside a square with two copies of the same relation, using a=ap and b=pb to place both copies in the component of w0. Conversely, if there is a self-intersection, Lemma 3.9 gives words with a repeated insertable block, yielding a violation of (iii); if there is self-osculation, Lemma 3.10 similarly yields an insertable block (k h)^{n+1} whose class is nontrivial, again violating (iii). Since Lemma 3.8 already gives 2-sidedness, this proves the equivalence. For specialness, after cleanliness only inter-osculation remains. The proof analyzes the two possible overlapping configurations of two edges with common endpoint: edges of the form (a,uv→p,wb) and (au,vw→q,b), or edges of the form (au,v→p,wb) and (a,uvw→q,b). Applying the transversality criterion Lemma 3.2 shows that each inter-osculation either violates condition (iii) or gives exactly the displayed obstruction involving xi. Conversely, if the displayed obstruction exists, the edges (a,uv→p,wb) and (au,vw→q,b) share the endpoint auvwb, do not lie in a common square, but their hyperplanes cross in the square (a,uv→p,xi,vw→q,b), so the hyperplanes inter-osculate. For the finiteness corollary, if a=ap and b=pb then w0 is congruent to a p^n b for all n, giving infinitely many representatives unless the insertion obstruction is absent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 (chains in ≺ are realized by cubes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J1,...,Jn be hyperplanes of S(P,w) such that Ji ≺ J_{i+1} for every i=1,...,n−1. Then there exists an n-cube (a1,u1→v1,a2,u2→v2,...,an,un→vn,a_{n+1}) in S(P,w) such that, for each k, the hyperplane dual to the kth direction is Jk; explicitly Jk = [a1 u1 ... a_{k−1} u_{k−1} a_k, u_k→v_k, a_{k+1} u_{k+1} ... a_n u_n a_{n+1}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n. The case n=2 is exactly the definition of J1 ≺ J2 as crossing in a square with J1 on the left. For the induction step, apply the hypothesis to J1,...,Jn to get an n-cube with Jn as its last direction. Since Jn ≺ J_{n+1}, there is a square (a,un→vn,b,p→q,c) in which Jn is the left hyperplane and J_{n+1} the right one. Lemma 3.1 identifies the prefix a with the prefix of the last direction in the existing n-cube and identifies b p c with the final suffix a_{n+1} modulo P. Replacing that suffix by b,p→q,c produces an (n+1)-cube. Lemma 3.1 again verifies that the first n directions still represent J1,...,Jn and that the new last direction represents J_{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3 / Proposition 1.3 (ordered intersections of Squier hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For hyperplanes of S(P,w), the relation ≺ has the following properties. First, if J1 ≺ J2 and J2 ≺ J3, then J1 ≺ J3. Second, two hyperplanes J1 and J2 are comparable with respect to ≺ if and only if they intersect. Third, the maximum length n of a chain J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn is equal to dim S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For transitivity, apply Lemma 4.2 to J1,J2,J3 to obtain a 3-cube with these as ordered coordinate directions; the square using only the first and third directions witnesses J1 ≺ J3. Comparability is built into the definition: whenever two hyperplanes meet in a square, one of the two possible left-right orders holds; and if one is ≺ the other, they meet in the defining square. For the dimension statement, any chain of length n gives an n-cube by Lemma 4.2, so dim S(P,w) ≥ n. Conversely, every n-cube has n ordered coordinate directions from left to right, and these hyperplanes form a chain J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn, so the maximum chain length is at least the dimension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.5 (restriction on the transversality graph)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The transversality graph of S(P,w), whose vertices are hyperplanes and whose edges join transverse hyperplanes, contains no induced cycle of odd length greater than five.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume an induced odd cycle J1,...,Jn with n≥5 and n odd. Choose an orientation of the first edge, say J1 ≺ J2. Since J2 crosses J3, either J2 ≺ J3 or J3 ≺ J2. The first option would imply J1 ≺ J3 by transitivity, hence J1 crosses J3, contradicting inducedness; therefore J3 ≺ J2. Continuing around the cycle forces the inequalities to alternate. Because n is odd, the final forced inequality is incompatible with closing the cycle: whichever orientation the crossing between Jn and J1 has, transitivity forces an extra crossing Jn with J2 or J1 with J_{n−1}, again contradicting that the cycle is induced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8 (equal-rank hyperplanes are disjoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a hyperplane J in S(P,w), define rank(J) = sup{n ≥ 0 such that there exist hyperplanes J1,...,Jn with J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn ≺ J}. The rank of a hyperplane in the Farley complex X(P,w) is defined as the rank of its image in S(P,w). If J1 and J2 are hyperplanes of X(P,w) with finite equal rank, then J1 and J2 are disjoint.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If J1 and J2 were transverse, their images in S(P,w) would be comparable by Corollary 4.3; suppose J1 ≺ J2. Let r=rank(J1) and choose a chain H1 ≺ ... ≺ Hr ≺ J1. Then H1 ≺ ... ≺ Hr ≺ J1 ≺ J2 is a chain of length r+1 below J2, so rank(J2)>rank(J1), contradicting equality. The other orientation is symmetric.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.16 (isometric embedding of the Farley complex into a product of rank trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume that S(P,w) has no infinite descending chain of hyperplanes with respect to ≺. Let J_k be the set of hyperplanes of the Farley complex X(P,w) having rank k. Since hyperplanes of the same rank are pairwise disjoint, cubulating the pocset of halfspaces determined by J_k gives a tree Lambda_k. Let Phi_k: X(P,w) → Lambda_k be the natural map sending a vertex to the principal ultrafilter it defines, and let Phi = Phi_0 × Phi_1 × ... . Then Phi embeds X(P,w) isometrically into the product Lambda_0 × Lambda_1 × ... with respect to combinatorial metrics.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 4.8, each rank class J_k is a family of pairwise disjoint hyperplanes, so the dual cube complex to its halfspace pocset is 1-dimensional; because it is CAT(0), it is a tree. For vertices x,y in X(P,w), Proposition 2.14 gives d_{Lambda_k}(Phi_k(x),Phi_k(y)) equal to the number of rank-k hyperplanes separating x and y. In the product of trees, the combinatorial distance is the sum over k of these numbers. The no-infinite-descending-chain hypothesis ensures every hyperplane has a finite rank, so the sum counts all hyperplanes separating x and y in X(P,w). Since combinatorial distance in a CAT(0) cube complex is the number of separating hyperplanes, d(Phi(x),Phi(y)) = d(x,y).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.21 (dimension criterion for Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n ≥ 1. The Squier complex S(P,w) has dimension at least n if and only if there exist words u1,...,un in Sigma+ such that w = u1 ... un modulo P and each congruence class [ui]_P is nontrivial, i.e. [ui]_P ≠ {ui}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If S(P,w) contains an n-cube (a1,p1→q1,...,an,pn→qn,a_{n+1}), then w is congruent to a product of n pieces, namely (a1 p1), ..., (a_{n−1} p_{n−1}), and (a_n p_n a_{n+1}), each of which has a nontrivial congruence class because the corresponding relation can be applied. Conversely, if w = u1 ... un modulo P and each [ui]_P is nontrivial, then inside each ui choose a subword pi on which a defining relation pi=qi applies, writing ui = xi pi yi. Applying all these n relations in the disjoint factors u1,...,un gives an n-cube (x1,p1→q1,y1 x2,p2→q2,...,y_{n−1} x_n,p_n→q_n,y_n) in S(P,w).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.5 and 5.6 (left hyperplanes are clean and mutually disjoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A hyperplane J=[a,u→v,b] of S(P,w) is called left if D(P,a) is trivial but D(P,a u) is nontrivial. Every left hyperplane is clean: it is 2-sided, non-self-intersecting, and non-self-osculating. Moreover, two left hyperplanes do not intersect each other.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a left hyperplane self-intersected, Lemma 3.9 and Lemma 3.1 would give a congruence a = a u c for some c, so D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a u c) ≃ D(P,a), contradicting D(P,a u) nontrivial and D(P,a) trivial. If it self-osculated, Lemma 3.10 gives u=(k h)^n k and a=a k h modulo P, so D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a(k h)^{n+1}) ≃ D(P,a), again a contradiction. For two left hyperplanes crossing in a square (a,u→v,b,p→q,c), the right hyperplane would be [a u b,p→q,c] while the left one is [a,u→v,b p c]; leftness of the right hyperplane gives D(P,a u b)=trivial, but D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a u b), contradicting leftness of the left hyperplane.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.7 (which edges remain after cutting along left hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J=[a,u→v,b] be a left hyperplane. Write u = p_J ell_J s_J, where ell_J is a letter, p_J and s_J are words, D(P,a p_J) is trivial, and D(P,a p_J ell_J) is nontrivial; p_J is the maximal prefix of u with this property. Let x,y,p,q be words with a u b = x p y as words and p=q a defining relation. Then the hyperplane H=[x,p→q,y] is not left if and only if the occurrence of p as a subword of a u b is contained entirely in a p_J or entirely in s_J b.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p lies inside a p_J, then D(P,x p) embeds into D(P,a p_J)=1, so H cannot be left. If p lies inside s_J b, then a p_J ell_J lies inside x, so D(P,a p_J ell_J) embeds into D(P,x), making D(P,x) nontrivial; again H is not left. Conversely, if p is contained in neither a p_J nor s_J b, then its occurrence must contain the distinguished letter ell_J. Thus x lies inside a p_J, giving D(P,x)=1, while a p_J ell_J embeds into x p, giving D(P,x p) nontrivial. This is exactly the definition that H is left.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8 (graph-of-spaces decomposition by left hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the set of left hyperplanes of S(P,w). For each left hyperplane J=[a,u→v,b], write u=p_J ell_J s_J and v=q_J m_J r_J as in Lemma 5.7, with the analogous maximal-prefix property on the v side. Define a graph of spaces whose vertex-spaces are the subcomplexes S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b) and S(P,a q_J) m_J S(P,r_J b), for all J=[a,u→v,b] in J, identifying equal vertex-spaces as one vertex. To each left hyperplane [a,u→v,b] attach the edge-space S(P,a) × S(P,b), with edge maps the canonical maps to S(P,a) u S(P,b) and S(P,a) v S(P,b). Then this graph of spaces has geometric realization S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Remove from S(P,w) the open carriers N(J)\∂J of all left hyperplanes J. Lemmas 5.5 and 5.6 imply these hyperplanes are clean and mutually disjoint, so the original Squier complex is recovered by gluing back each carrier, which by Theorem 3.5 is S(P,a)×S(P,b)×[0,1] with boundary faces S(P,a)uS(P,b) and S(P,a)vS(P,b). It remains to identify the connected components of the cut complex. For the component C_u containing a u b, prove C_u = S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b). One inclusion is by induction on the combinatorial distance from a u b to the initial point of an edge e in C_u. At distance zero, Lemma 5.7 says any edge not dual to a left hyperplane must apply its relation entirely inside a p_J or inside s_J b, hence lies in the product subcomplex. For the induction step, the previous edge gives a vertex alpha ell_J beta with alpha congruent to a p_J and beta congruent to s_J b; if the next relation crossed ell_J, the same embedding argument as in Lemma 5.7 would make it a left hyperplane, impossible in the cut component. Thus it again lies entirely on one side. The reverse inclusion follows from Lemma 5.7 because no edge of S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b) is dual to a left hyperplane. The v side is symmetric. This identifies all vertices and edge gluings of the graph of spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Mark a local move and classify its hyperplane by context
proof_techniques.description: In a Squier complex, an edge is a local rewriting u→v inside a left and right context, written (a,u→v,b). Lemma 3.1 shows that the associated oriented hyperplane is determined exactly by the central relation u→v and by the two contexts a,b modulo the semigroup presentation. The proof works by commuting all other independent rewrites past the marked rewrite through squares, so that moving within the hyperplane changes only the contexts modulo P and never the marked relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one could try to mark a single crossing or local resolution and define hyperplanes as equivalence classes under commuting resolutions of other crossings. A useful first target is an analogue of Lemma 3.1: identify precisely when two marked crossings in different matchings represent the same hyperplane.

proof_techniques.name: Order transverse hyperplanes by left-right position
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines J1 ≺ J2 when two hyperplanes meet in a square and J1 corresponds to the rewrite in the left subword while J2 corresponds to the rewrite in the right subword. Lemma 4.2 proves that any chain J1≺...≺Jn is realized simultaneously as the n ordered coordinate directions of a single cube. Corollary 4.3 then gives transitivity, comparability exactly when hyperplanes intersect, and equality between maximum chain length and dimension.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The matching complex has an ambient linear order on boundary points and planar arcs, so crossings or resolution events may admit a natural left-right or nesting order. Proving a chain-to-cell lemma analogous to Lemma 4.2 would be a strong route to flag links, CAT(0)-type structure, or an inductive dimension control.

proof_techniques.name: Use hyperplane pathologies as explicit forbidden local configurations
proof_techniques.description: Rather than checking specialness abstractly, the paper translates self-intersection, self-osculation, and inter-osculation of Squier hyperplanes into concrete algebraic patterns: repeated insertable blocks for self-intersection, overlapping periodic words for self-osculation, and a specific two-relation overlap for inter-osculation. Theorem 3.6 then proves clean/special criteria by showing that all possible pathologies are exactly these patterns.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced hypothesis in the original problem forbids triples ell ell^{-1} ell. A promising transfer is to classify possible self-osculations or non-flag link configurations in F_w and check whether quasi-reducedness is precisely the local condition excluding them.

proof_techniques.name: Product neighborhoods of hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.5 describes the carrier boundary of a Squier hyperplane [a,u→v,b] as S(P,a)uS(P,b) on one side and S(P,a)vS(P,b) on the other; when the hyperplane is clean, the hyperplane itself is isometric to S(P,a)×S(P,b). This product structure comes from the fact that all moves independent of the marked rewrite occur either in the left context or in the right context.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Resolving or fixing an arc/crossing in a matching often separates the word into subintervals or regions. If the subcomplex around such a feature factors into independent inside/outside matching complexes, this can support an induction on word length or number of pairs.

proof_techniques.name: Cubulate a halfspace pocset to prove CAT(0) and contractibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls Sageev-Roller cubulation: from a pocset of halfspaces one builds a CAT(0) cube complex whose vertices are DCC ultrafilters. Principal ultrafilters from a CAT(0) cube complex map into this cubulation, and Proposition 2.14 says distances are counted by the selected separating hyperplanes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If vertices of F_w can be encoded as consistent choices of sides of hyperplanes or choices of resolutions satisfying a DCC/consistency condition, cubulation could identify F_w or its universal cover with a CAT(0) cube complex. CAT(0) would immediately imply contractibility.

proof_techniques.name: Rank-color hyperplanes and embed into products of trees
proof_techniques.description: Using the order ≺, the paper defines the rank of a hyperplane as the length of the longest descending chain below it. Lemma 4.8 shows that hyperplanes of the same finite rank are pairwise disjoint. Each rank class therefore dualizes to a tree, and Theorem 4.16 embeds the Farley complex isometrically into the product of these trees by recording the side of every rank-k hyperplane.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w has an ordered hyperplane structure with no infinite descending chains, ranks could provide a coloring by noncrossing families. This may help prove median or CAT(0) behavior, or at least give a controlled normal form for paths by counting separating hyperplanes.

proof_techniques.name: Cut along a clean disjoint family and reconstruct a graph of spaces
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.8 selects left hyperplanes, proves they are clean and mutually disjoint, removes their open carriers, identifies the components of the complement via a maximal-prefix argument, and then reconstructs the whole Squier complex as a graph of spaces. The edge spaces are product hyperplanes, and the vertex spaces are smaller Squier-type subcomplexes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one might choose a family of outermost or leftmost arcs/crossings that are pairwise disjoint in the hyperplane sense. Cutting along them could reduce contractibility to smaller matching complexes glued along product subcomplexes, with the nerve possibly a tree.

proof_techniques.name: Induction by maximal trivial prefix
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 5.7 and Theorem 5.8, the paper finds the first letter in a word prefix where the diagram group changes from trivial to nontrivial. This distinguished letter acts as a separator: any edge crossing it is necessarily dual to a left hyperplane, while edges not crossing it remain in one of two product factors. This gives an exact description of components after cutting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar separator could be the first boundary point or first matched pair at which a reducible subword becomes nontrivial. Such a separator may allow an inductive decomposition of F_w into subcomplexes corresponding to the left and right parts of the word.

proof_techniques.name: Use transversality graph restrictions as diagnostics
proof_techniques.description: Because the Squier hyperplane intersection relation is ordered, Corollary 4.5 rules out induced odd cycles of length greater than five in the transversality graph. The proof is purely order-theoretic: an induced cycle forces alternating inequalities, and odd length makes the alternation inconsistent with transitivity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed hyperplane structure on F_w has a transversality graph with forbidden induced odd cycles, this supports an ordered-Squier-like model. Conversely, proving such an order may simplify link-flag checks by reducing possible obstruction cycles.

other_useful_info: Corollary 3.7 is especially concise: if the congruence class [w]_P is finite, then S(P,w) is special; the introduction phrases this as every compact Squier complex is special. Remark 4.6 notes that the no-induced-odd-cycle restriction is sharp only for odd cycles: induced even cycles can occur in Squier transversality graphs. Theorem 5.13 constructs right-angled Artin groups from complements of finite interval graphs as diagram groups, and Theorem 5.16 cites the characterization that a graph is the complement of an interval graph iff it has no induced C4 and is transitively orientable; this may be relevant only if a hyperplane crossing graph arising from matchings turns out to be interval-graph-like. The paper repeatedly uses the standard fact that combinatorial distance in a CAT(0) cube complex equals the number of separating hyperplanes, a useful metric criterion for any attempted cubical model of F_w.
why_relevant: Analyzes hyperplanes and decompositions of Squier cube complexes. If F_w admits a cubical or median structure, these hyperplane methods could help prove nonpositive curvature or contractibility.
